titleEn: O sons of Sulaym, if you meet Faqas
translationEn: O sons of Sulaym, if you meet the Faqas clan in a narrow pass leading to rough terrain, meet them with your swords and spears, and with a night raid like the pouring rain, until you scatter their assembly and remember Sakhr and how he was slain without vengeance being taken. And remember the horsemen of ours who were killed there in a stumble that fate dealt. He met Rabia in battle and was struck with a wound that pierced through to his chest — a straight spear with soft joints at its nodes, its blade keen as the eagle's leading talon. Rabia escaped that day in haste, running without slackening in his stride. A lean swift horse like an eagle swooping at dawn carried him. We took Khalid captive, and Awf granted him protection and released him in due measure. Our counsel intervened rightly in the matter of Khalid, something that would shame the horses to the end of time.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Faqas, a clan of the Asad tribe (enemies)
a narrow confining place, a tight pass
a night raid, a surprise attack
a wound piercing deep into the body cavity
flexible at the joints (said of a spear shaft)
sharp, keen (of a blade)
hard-pressed, pursued in haste
to slacken, to hold back

context: This poem is an incitement (tahrid) calling the Sulaym tribe to avenge the death of Sakhr, Al-Khansa's brother, who was killed without retribution. It also alludes to the capture and release of a man named Khalid, weaving tribal politics into the elegy's call for vengeance.

titleAr: يا عين فيضي بدمع منك مغزار
titleEn: O eye, pour forth abundant tears

verses.sadr: يا عين فيضي بدمع منك مغزار
وقد سمعت فلم أبهج به خبرا
قال ابن امك ثاو بالضريح وقد
فاذهب فلا يبعدنك الله من رجل
قد كنت تحمل قلبا غير مهتضم
لو منكم كان فينا لم ينل ابدا
و ابكوا فتى البأس وافته منيته
لا نوم حتى تقودوا الخيل عابسة

verses.ajuz: حلت على طبق من ظهرها عار
راموا الشكيمة من ذي لبدة ضار
ماض على الهول هاد غير محيار

translationEn: O my eye, pour forth tears in abundant floods, and weep for Sakhr with a pouring, ceaseless flow. I lay awake and spent the night in wakefulness, as though my eyes were rubbed with raw inflammation. I watch the stars without having taken on their tending, and at times I wrap myself in the excess of my garments. I heard a piece of news that brought me no joy — a messenger stood up spreading the tidings again and again: 'Your mother's son lies in his tomb, and they have covered him with planks and stones.' Depart then — may God not distance you — O man who repelled oppression and sought blood-vengeance. You bore a heart that was not crushed, set in a foundation that did not waver, like a spear-point whose form lights up the night, patient in hardship, a free man and son of free men. I weep for the young man of the tribe whom death has overtaken, for every soul has its appointed time and measure. I shall weep for you as long as the ring-dove coos and the night stars light the way for the traveller. I will make no peace with people who were your enemies until pitch turns white. Convey to the Sulaym and Awf, if you meet them, a message openly declared and not in secret: I mean those with whom he dwelled — do you know the sacred bond of guest and neighbour? Had he been among us, he would never have been harmed until matters of great consequence came to pass. He was as your cousin and your guest among you, yet you did not defend him — a breach of trust. Tighten your belts until the affair is settled for you, and gird yourselves, for these are days that demand girding. Weep for the warrior whose death came to him amid every calamity that befell and every decree. No sleep until you lead the horses grim-faced, casting down foals and mares in battle. Or dig a grave — for death lurks close by the houses of Husayn and Ibn Sayar. Or wash away the shame that has wrapped you, as menstrual cloths are washed clean at the time of purity. War has been ridden hard, like a humpbacked runaway camel, crouching upon a broad back left bare. It was as though all of them together, when they attempted him, attempted to seize the bit of a maned, deadly lion — guardian of his lair in battle, powerful, tearing men with fangs and claws — until the thousands scattered before a man who pressed forward through terror, a guide who never wavered, with a deep wound above his chest, bubbling with foaming dark blood from within.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: abundant, copious (of tears)
pouring constantly, incessant
lying still, dwelling (in the grave)
one who repels oppression / injustice
the black tar-box (proverbially black; 'until it turns white')
the sacred bond of protection owed to a guest or neighbour
broad-shouldered, powerful, capable
dark, flowing blood

context: One of Al-Khansa's longest and most powerful elegies for Sakhr, combining personal grief, sleepless nights, and a fierce call to the Sulaym tribe to avenge his death. It is notable for its sustained battle imagery, concluding with a vivid portrait of a lone warrior still standing though mortally wounded.

titleAr: عين فابكي لي على صخر إذا
titleEn: O eye, weep for me over Sakhr

verses.sadr: عين فابكي لي على صخر إذا

translationEn: O eye, weep for me over Sakhr whenever the blade rises above the haunches of the slaughtered camels, when the people are filled with fat whenever the wind bends the branches of the trees. And when the white women walk together like water-birds in the shallow, murky pool, leaning beneath the tips of the spears, their shins visible in a cautious mountain pass — he would deal a thrust that no healer's incantation could stanch, nor any bandaging of palm fibres.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the humps/backs of the slaughtered camels
bent, twisted (the wind bending branches)
water-birds (a simile for graceful women)
the shallow, turbid pool
leaning, inclining to one side
to stanch, to stop the flow (of blood)

context: A short, vivid elegy in which Al-Khansa links the memory of Sakhr to scenes of tribal generosity — the slaughter of camels to feed guests — and battle prowess, praising his lethal spear-thrust as something no remedy could heal.

titleAr: كأن ابن عمر و لم يصبح لغارة
titleEn: As though ibn Amr never rode to raid

verses.sadr: كأن ابن عمر و لم يصبح لغارة
فبكوا على صخر بن عمر و فإنه
verses.ajuz: يسير اذا ما الدهر بالناس اعسرا

translationEn: It is as though ibn Amr never set out at dawn for a raid with cavalry, nor drove lean, swift thoroughbred she-camels hard. As if he never crossed the land with true companions, shrouded in the dust that hooves kicked up into a dark cloud. As if he never built shade for his young men in the fierce midday heat — a decorated, embroidered mantle of shadow. Weep then for Sakhr ibn Amr, for he was easy-going when fate pressed hard upon people. He gave freely and was sweet when his goodness was sought, yet bitter as bitter colocynth when someone sought to provoke his bitterness. So al-Khansa weeps in the darkness, sorrowful, calling out to her brother who does not answer, lying face-down in the dust.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: noble, swift she-camels bred for riding
lean, spare (of camels trained for speed)
dust raised by hooves in battle or a raid
the fierce midday heats of summer
became difficult, pressed hard (of fate)
intensely bitter (like colocynth)
face-down in the dust, prostrated

context: A poignant elegy in which Al-Khansa imagines the world as if Sakhr had never existed — stripping away all his great deeds — before ending with the intimate image of herself, al-Khansa, weeping alone in the dark and calling out to her dead brother.

titleEn: O eye, pour forth your tears
translationEn: O my eye, be generous with tears for the noble, glorious youth — bright-faced and radiant as the sun, the finest of mankind. Even the sun is eclipsed at his perishing, and the moon is no longer full. Mankind weeps and wails for him, and the jinn cry out through the night. Even the wild beasts weep their grief when the news of him arrived. He was the great spokesman, the overflowing one, who bore the burdens of his clan upon his shoulders. He gave generously and never boasted of it, and hardheartedness was never his character. Woe upon woe is mine for him — I have become a fortress whose ramparts are broken.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the chief, the master (of men)
the glorious, bright-faced, illustrious
bright-faced, radiant, clear-browed
the chief spokesman, the foremost defender of his tribe
the overflowing one, the immensely generous
the abundant gift, lavish generosity
natural character, innate disposition

context: Al-Khansa mourns a great chieftain whose death is felt by all creation — humans, jinn, and wild beasts alike — a hyperbolic device common in pre-Islamic elegy, exalting the deceased's cosmic significance. The poem closes with the poetess herself as a broken fortress, her protector gone.

titleEn: Grief and sleeplessness visited me

verses.sadr: تبكي لصخر وقد راب الزمان به

verses.ajuz: وافي الذمام إذا ما معشر غدروا
عند المحول إذ ما هبت القرر

verses.sadr: ما بارز القرن يوما عند معركة
verses.ajuz: الا له يوم تسمو الكرة الظفر

translationEn: Sorrows and sleeplessness came and lodged with me; my eye flows in stillness with tears like pearls. She weeps for Sakhr, for time has turned suspicious against him — the accidents of days and fate consumed him. His character was generous, his gifts were ample, and he was true to his bond when others around him betrayed. He was the shelter of the destitute and the refuge of every widow in times of drought when the cold winds blew. Never did he face an opponent in battle without that day becoming for him a day when the tide of victory surged to triumph.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: came and settled with me (said of grief visiting one)
pearls (simile for beautiful, precious tears)
time became troubled/suspicious, turned against
the destitute, the one in need
years of drought, year of no rain
cold winds, the cold season
the counter-attack, the tide turning in battle

context: A short, intimate elegy cataloguing Sakhr's virtues as a protector of the weak — widows, the destitute — and as an invincible warrior, structured around the classic elegiac movement from grief to praise.

titleEn: My eyes, shed tears unstintingly

verses.sadr: يا صخر ! من لطراد الخيل إذ وزعت
ومن لكربة عان في الوثاق ، ومن
فر الاقارب عنها بعد ما ضربوا

verses.ajuz: من بعد لذة عيش غير مقتور

verses.sadr: يا صخر كنت لنا عيشا نعيش به
يا فارس الخيل إن شدوا فلم يهنوا
يا لهف نفسي على صخر إذا ركبت
يا صخر ماذا يواري القبر من كرم

translationEn: My two eyes, be generous with tears unstopped, and wail! For Sakhr is the best of those laid in graves. Do not fail me, for I shall never forget the memory of Sakhr, companion of glory and goodness. O Sakhr! Who now will drive the horses when they are spread for the chase, and tend the riding-camels when their saddles are strapped? Who is there for the orphans and the guests who arrive at our houses at night, known for deeds that were yours? And who for the anguish of a prisoner in bonds, who gave lavishly in hardship and in ease? Who now for the steady spear-thrust or the cry of a woman calling for help on the day of battle's clamour, when the raiders' horsemen ride? The near kinsmen fled after striking with the Mashrafi swords — a striking that was no warning blow. She was abandoned after the clashing of white blades, and had to make her hard way after the sweet life that was never stinted. O Sakhr, you were a life we lived by, had the calamities of fate only left you to us. O horseman of the cavalry when they charged without wavering, and horseman of the people when they intended to fall short. O how my soul aches for Sakhr, when horses charge against horses swift as oryx! He kindled war — a war that only firestarters, sons of firestarters, can kindle. O Sakhr, what does the grave conceal of nobility and characters pure and immaculate!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: stinted, held back, restrained
were spread out, distributed (horses in a chase)
the saddle (of a camel)
a prisoner, a captive
persistent, steady (said of a long thrust)
swords from the Mashrif region, renowned for quality
those who kindle fire; warriors who ignite war
pure, immaculate, morally clean

context: A long, searching elegy in which Al-Khansa piles up a series of rhetorical questions — 'who now will do what Sakhr did?' — to catalogue his indispensable roles as protector, warrior, and provider, before ending with the image of the grave hiding his boundless nobility.

titleAr: يا عين جودي بدمع غير منزور
titleEn: O eye, weep without restraint

verses.sadr: يا عين جودي بدمع غير منزور
نعم الفتى كنت إذ حنت مرفرفة

translationEn: O eye, be generous with tears unstopped, like pearls rolling down the cheeks. Weep for a brother whose qualities were praiseworthy, radiant like the crescent moon, not obscured. The horseman of the cavalry — fate came to him, and in my heart is a fracture that cannot be mended. What a fine young man you were, when the fluttering wild doves moaned with the moaning of mad love in the bewildered oryx! And the horses stumble over the heroes, grim-faced, like wolves that leap and wallow in the dust.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pearls (simile for tears rolling on cheeks)
rolling down, descending
obscured, hidden, overshadowed
a crack/fracture that cannot be set or healed
a fluttering dove; one who flaps its wings
mad with grief or love, bereaved
wolves (plural of سرحان)

context: A brief elegy comparing Sakhr to the luminous crescent moon and concluding with images of battlefield carnage, the grief of bereaved doves mirroring Al-Khansa's own ceaseless mourning.

titleEn: O eye, pour forth abundant tears

verses.ajuz: في إثر غاد سار حد النهار
من كان من ذي رحم أو جوار

verses.sadr: إن يك هذا الدهر أودى به

translationEn: O my eye, pour forth abundant tears and weep over the awe-inspiring guardian of all that is sacred. A branch from the most generous of peoples, raised among them by every man of pure lineage. When the news of his death reached me and people openly disclosed what had been whispered in secret, I said: 'My brother! If you have taken your leave of us, you were a branch from a people noble in ancestry. How many a kindness you bestowed upon families and young orphans! How many a favour you granted to captives, shackled in bondage!' May my family be ransom for him whose bones lie gleaming among the stony ground — felled by spears and sharp-honed blades flashing like lightning among the dwellings. Whoever would weep one day for a great lord, let him weep for him with hot, scalding tears. Let the horses weep for him when they are left on the field of death on the morning of disaster. Let every man of anguish whose refuge has grown too narrow weep for him — a spring of rain in destruction, a refuge of dew when people fear drought. May the land that holds his grave be watered by the first spring rains and the rushing clouds. And my prayer for that is only so that the skulls in the wilderness may be quenched with water. Say to him who gloats over his fall: you and death are wrapped together in one cloak. What consoles my grief is that whoever rejoiced at his death will follow him — do not doubt it. Between them is but an evening's journey, following a morning traveller who set out at midday. O you who struck the horseman in battle with the sword in the heat-shimmering fray! He brought him down on the horse's neck, a sleek swift mount like a wolf, sure-footed in the charge. He fought heroes who had defenders until they turned back from the sacred bonds of honour. I swear by the House and its pilgrims as they drive their white camels towards the stone pillars: I shall never grieve for any man who has died after you, as long as the pregnant she-camels call out. O the anguish, its torment is manifest, striking sparks of grief in my heart like burning embers! Those who were kin or neighbours have shown me coldness since his departure. If fate has done away with him and reduced him to dust traced over by the running waters — yet every living thing is destined for decay, and every rope of strength must one day unravel.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: awe-inspiring, one who strikes with wonder
guardian of what is sacred and inviolable
captives, prisoners (plural of عانٍ)
shackled, bound tightly in captivity
the first spring rains (the most nourishing)
torments of grief, agonies of passion
unravelling, disintegration, being buried and forgotten

context: One of Al-Khansa's most extended and philosophically rich elegies, moving from personal grief to consolation through the universality of death, and containing a remarkable sworn oath that she will mourn no other after Sakhr. The poem ends on a stoic note about the inevitability of decay.

titleEn: O Sakhr, who will face fate's trials
translationEn: O Sakhr! Who now is there for the trials of fate? Or who will make easy the path of the one riding through rough terrain? You were the one who relieved whatever afflicted us — yet now I find no sweetness and no bitterness from you. Dust is heaped upon his fair features and upon the freshness of his radiant face.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the accidents and misfortunes of fate
the rider of rough, difficult terrain
the one who brings relief, who eases hardship
whatever afflicts, whatever calamity befalls
you neither give sweetness nor give milk — proverbial for producing nothing
freshness, bloom, youthfulness

context: A very short but striking fragment mourning Sakhr's irreplaceable role as the tribe's problem-solver, using the proverbial phrase 'neither sweet nor bitter' to convey the emptiness left by his death.

titleEn: You called on Amir but cast him aside
translationEn: You called on Amir but then abandoned him, and you did not call on Muawiya ibn Amr. Had you called out to him, he would have come to you running, swift in his gallop, or he would have come at full stride — proud when the spears clashed and crossed, avenging every injury with a counterstrike. When he met death, he did not care whether it came to him in ease or in hardship, like a lion crouching with its forepaws spread, bold-chested, a great stealthy hunter.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you cast him aside, abandoned him
swift in running, at a rapid pace
proud, self-assured, striding boldly
when the spear-shafts clash and interlock
crouching with forepaws spread (of a lion ready to pounce)
a great, cunning hunter (said of a lion)
stretched out, stalking with long strides

context: This elegy mourns Muawiya ibn Amr, Al-Khansa's other brother, criticising the tribe for turning to the wrong man for help while this fearless warrior was available. It is one of the few poems primarily centred on Muawiya rather than Sakhr.

titleEn: We were like night stars with a moon among them

verses.ajuz: يجلو الدجى فهوى من بيننا القمر

verses.sadr: يا صخر ! ما كنت في قوم أسر بهم
فاذهب حميدا على ما كان من حدث

translationEn: We were like the stars of the night with a moon at their centre, dispelling darkness — and then the moon fell from among us. O Sakhr! Whenever you were among a people that delighted me, you were always the one most famed among them. Depart then, praised for all that came to pass, for you have trodden a path that holds a lesson for all.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: dispelling the darkness (of night)
fell, plummeted (the moon falling from the sky)
renowned, famous, the one known to all
praised, honoured, lauded
a lesson, a source of moral reflection

context: A brief but gem-like elegy using the central image of a moon falling from among stars — one of the most memorable similes in Al-Khansa's poetry — to convey Sakhr's irreplaceable centrality to his tribe.

titleEn: We were like two branches of a tall tree

verses.ajuz: حينا على خير ما ينمى له الشجر

verses.sadr: حتى اذا قيل قد طالت عروقهما
أخنى على واحد ريب الزمان ، وما
verses.ajuz: يبقي الزمان على شيء ولا يذر

translationEn: We were like two branches growing tall from a single root, for a time in the finest growth a tree could produce. Until it was said that their roots had grown deep and long, and their planting had ripened, and the fruit had rounded fully — then fate's calamity fell upon one of them. For time spares nothing and leaves nothing untouched.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the root stock, the base of a tree
tall and lofty, high-growing (of a tree or branch)
the fruit gathered and rounded, becoming complete
brought ruin and destruction (said of fate or time)
the treacheries and misfortunes of time

context: A philosophical mini-elegy using the image of two flourishing branches cut down by time's cruelty, ending with one of Al-Khansa's most quoted observations on the indiscriminate destruction wrought by fate.

titleAr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك مدرار
titleEn: O eye, pour forth streaming tears

verses.sadr: يا عين جودي بدمع منك مدرار

verses.ajuz: كالبدر يجلو ولا يخفى على الساري

translationEn: O my eye, be generous with streaming tears, straining in lamentation like the water of a running stream. Weep for your brother and forget not his qualities, weep for your brother who was brave and never craven. Weep for your brother — for the orphans and the widow, weep for your brother for the right of the guest and the neighbour. His generosity was abundant, his fingertips always moist with giving; like the full moon he shone and was never hidden from the night traveller. A returner of loans and a ransomer of captive women, like a ferocious lion, crushing its opponent. A crosser of valleys, a bearer of banners in war, open-handed and generous without stint.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pouring incessantly, torrential
loud lamentation, wailing in grief
one who returns borrowed items faithfully
one who ransoms captive women
a lion (a poetic epithet)
one who seizes and crushes, a crusher (of an opponent)
a bearer of battle standards

context: A catalogue elegy enumerating Sakhr's virtues in parallel phrases — his generosity, bravery, care for the weak, and prowess in battle — each verse adding another dimension to the portrait of the ideal warrior-chieftain.

titleAr: الا ابكي على صخر وصخر ثمالنا
titleEn: Weep for Sakhr, our mainstay in battle

verses.sadr: الا ابكي على صخر وصخر ثمالنا
من الهضبة العليا التي ليس كالصفا

verses.ajuz: منيع الذرى عال على من يثيرها
إذا مل عنها ذات يوم ضجورها
به عن حيال ملقح من يبورها

translationEn: Weep for Sakhr — and Sakhr was our mainstay whenever war bared its fangs and its bitterness ran strong. He steadied war's two wings and they joined forces against its hard blows until even its most difficult aspects were set right, in a flashing engagement where death dwelt in a cloud of dust, its shoulders and its throats envenomed. He rushed upon it while blood was flowing and its thunder was the murmur of heroes seldom slackening. For Sakhr in its midst was the speaker for all of war, and Sakhr when men betrayed, he swept it into flight. From the highest plateau whose smooth stone is unlike any other flatness, and whose boulders are unlike any other boulders — it has battlements none can reach and an impregnable shoulder, lofty in its peaks, towering over any who might try to stir it. His were two expanses of glory: one hand that gave abundantly, and another whose spear-tips were its jagged ridges. War had reared him and he did not tire of it, even when the weary grew disgusted and turned away. When war clouded dark for the night assault, it was certain through him, and from his firm seat it inseminated whoever was barren of it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: our support, our mainstay, our refuge
snarled, bared its fangs (said of war like a beast)
its bitterness, its fierceness (of war)
the champion spokesman, the foremost fighter for his tribe
the lofty plateau — extended metaphor for Sakhr's greatness
darkened, frowned, grew heavy and threatening
makes barren what was fruitless (rare usage of بار)

context: A highly complex elegy which develops an extended metaphor comparing Sakhr to an unassailable mountain plateau, piling up images of war and dominance. The poem is celebrated for its unusual and vivid military imagery.

titleEn: Time has gnawed at me with biting and cutting

verses.ajuz: اذ الناس اذ ذاك من عزبزا
بان لا يصاب فقد ظن عجزا

translationEn: Time has gnawed at me with biting and cutting, and time has pained me with striking and pressing. It wiped out my men, who rushed together to their doom, leaving my heart troubled and unsettled on their account. It is as if they had never been a protection held in awe, when people in those days were strong through them. They were the nobles of the Banu Malik, the ornament of the clan in generosity and in might. In ancient times they were the healers of the destitute, and the shelter from all fear. They protected their neighbour and the women whose insides were driven by terror. On the morning when they met them with a massive, well-arrayed force that left hollows in the earth — with white flat-bladed swords and brown spear-shafts: striking with the blades and piercing with the shafts — and cavalry pressing upon the armoured men, trotting rapidly under the dust-cloud. Whoever among those who meet wars thinks he will never be struck, he has thought a weakling's thought. We refrain from wrong and know the right of hospitality, and we take praise as our treasure and our hoard. We wear in battle the weaving of iron, and trail in peace the silk and fine linen.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gnawed at me, stripped me to the bone (like gnawing flesh from a bone)
biting and cutting (two kinds of gnawing)
restless, agitated, unsettled
a well-gathered, tightly-assembled army
heavy, massive and full (of an army)
broad, flat-bladed swords
trotting rapidly, cantering

context: A broader tribal elegy lamenting the loss of many men — not just Sakhr — and celebrating the tribe's code of honour: protecting neighbours, giving hospitality, and wearing armour in battle while displaying fine clothes in peace.

titleEn: O sons of Sulaym, weep for your horseman

verses.ajuz: وفارسا لا يرى مثل له راس

translationEn: O sons of Sulaym, will you not weep for your horseman who has left you affairs full of tangled ropes? What ails death that it comes at us morning and night as though we are forever being cut with an axe? It comes upon us every morning and refuses to leave us; all our good men are pledged to the grave. There will always be a young man newly come to manhood cut down, and a horseman whose equal is seen nowhere — taken from among us by surprise. Had strength alone been able to protect him, we would have found him living still, a man of prowess.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: affairs of tangled ropes — complex, difficult matters
comes upon us in the morning, attacks us at dawn
we are being cut with the axe (slaughtered in numbers)
pledged to graves, destined for the tomb
a young man newly come of age
took him by surprise, caught him unawares

context: Al-Khansa addresses the Sulaym tribe directly, calling them to mourn their fallen horseman and expressing outrage at the relentlessness of death, which takes young men and great warriors without ceasing.

titleEn: Memory keeps me sleepless at evening
translationEn: Remembrance keeps me sleepless when evening comes, and I wake afflicted with extreme despondency — over Sakhr. And what young man compares to Sakhr for a day of terrible battle and persistent thrusting, or for standing against the most stubborn adversary when he transgresses, to take the right of the wronged by force? I have seen no loss like him among all the jinn, and I have seen no loss like him among all mankind — more steadfast against the blows of fate, more decisive in crises without confusion. And a late-night guest or a man seeking protection, his heart startled by every sound — he would honour him and give him security so that by evening his mind was free of all misery. The rising of the sun reminds me of Sakhr, and I remember him at every setting of the sun. Were it not for the multitude of those weeping around me for their own brothers, I would have taken my own life. But I still see everywhere a bereaved woman and a weeping one wailing for a day of ill omen — I see her, bewildered, weeping for her brother on the evening of his loss or the day after. They weep for none who compare to my brother, yet I console my soul with joining in their grief. By God, I shall never forget you until I depart from my very soul and my grave is split open. On the day of parting from Sakhr, father of Hassan, I bade farewell to my pleasures and my comfort. How I grieve for him, and how my mother grieves — does he spend his mornings in the tomb and his evenings in it too?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: keeps me sleepless, causes insomnia
relapse, despondency, extreme weakness of spirit
a dreadful, hateful day (of battle)
persistent, continuous thrusting (in battle)
the most stubbornly contentious adversary
consoling oneself by noting that others share the same grief
the tomb, the grave

context: One of Al-Khansa's most personal and extended elegies, famous for its confession that only the sight of others weeping for their own loved ones kept her from taking her life. The poem moves between cosmic time (sunrise and sunset reminding her of Sakhr) and intimate domestic grief.

titleEn: O eye, weep for a horseman
translationEn: O my eye, weep for a horseman who was excellent in the thrust from horseback — a man of firm resolve and awe-inspiring presence; while we still hoped for him, he was snatched away. While we still saw him before us protecting his battalion fiercely, like a lion that leaps lightly to its lair, guarding its prey with surly defiance. He would leave the armoured warrior felled, his collar-bones dusty, knocked backward. He dyed the spearhead with a thrust, and breath drove out the last breath. The birds circled between those who approached and those who swooped to snatch. What a fine young man he was in battle, when the shouting rose in the dim of early dawn. I shall weep for you as a lord who decided all disputed speech when it grew confused. Who shall stand in his place after my mother's son was laid in his grave? Who shall return with his forbearance at the quarrel over hardship? He was the rain for the entire clan — those who raid and those who stay at home.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fierce, harsh, roughly combative
felled, thrown down to the ground
knocked backward, prostrated
one breath drives out the next (the death rattle)
swooping to snatch (of a bird of prey)
the darkness just before dawn, the pre-dawn dimness
the decisive word, the judge who settles disputes

context: An elegy combining vivid battle imagery — the fallen warrior, birds of prey circling the dead — with praise of Sakhr's wisdom as an arbitrator of disputes and as the sustaining rain of his entire clan.

titleAr: إن الزمان وما يفنى له عجب
titleEn: Time and its ceaseless consumption

verses.sadr: إن الزمان وما يفنى له عجب

translationEn: Truly time and what it consumes is a wonder — it leaves us the tail and swallows the head. It leaves us every unknown and worthless thing, yet bereaves us of the wise and thoughtful men, who are now skulls and graves. The two new things — day and night — in their long alternation never wear out themselves, yet they wear out mankind.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: what it annihilates is a wonder — remarkable for its destruction
swallowed/consumed the head (the best, the foremost)
the wise, the forbearing, men of sound judgment
skulls (plural of هامة)
the two new things — day and night — a classical poetic term

context: A rare philosophical (hikma) poem by Al-Khansa meditating on time's paradox: it destroys the best while leaving the worthless behind. The final verse about day and night is one of the most cited aphorisms in early Arabic poetry.

titleEn: As for the nights when I was a young girl
translationEn: As for the nights when I was a young girl, I was surrounded by watchmen and those who sat guard. Until when the curtained litter revealed me and the men cast me aside like the cast-off of one who departed seated — guarded by an ugly-faced neighbour woman keeping watch over me, and a protector who collapses like a cast-off worn carpet.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a young girl, a maiden
watchers, guardians, those keeping vigilance
the curtained litter or private enclosure of a woman
dismissively, cast aside like something worthless
ugly-faced, ill-omened in appearance
a worn, rough carpet or blanket (simile for a useless protector)

context: A rare personal poem in which Al-Khansa reflects on her youth and the inadequate protection she received — surrounded by watchmen and an ugly guardian — with implied comparison to the true protection that Sakhr once provided.

titleEn: O eye, help me in my grief
translationEn: O my eye, woe upon you — help me against the treacheries of time and its biting grip. Reserve no tears after Sakhr — you have been tasked all your life to overflow. Pour out tears over a noble man whom the calamities of time have struck — do not let them dry up. Since that young man of Sulaym I have sought to relieve the grief of my chest through poetry. I ask every bewildered, grief-stricken woman worn by time to the thinness of a broken bone. I no longer count myself as bodily sound, nor am I so sick as to be called ill. But at night, at the memory of Sakhr, I choke even on the cool, running water. I remember him when the earth has darkened into a vast wilderness with no flash of light. Who is there for war when it grows grim and those who kindle it have girded themselves to rise? And cavalry that advanced against another cavalry whose numbers were like the slope of the lowest cliff — when the people are stirred to vengeance they seek it, and blood-vengeance is pursued like a debt. With every sharpened Indian sword, keen and polished, fine of edge and bright.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the biting, gnawing (of time)
calamitous events, the blows of fate
a woman who has lost many children, grief-stricken
broken and not properly set (of a bone)
to choke, to be unable to swallow
vast, empty, desolate (of a landscape at night)
an Indian-made sword, of the finest quality

context: A long elegy notable for its intensely personal and physical description of grief — Al-Khansa cannot drink water without choking at Sakhr's memory — before pivoting to his indispensability in war, a classic movement from inner anguish to external heroic praise.

titleAr: لقد صوت الناعي بفقد اخي الندى
titleEn: The announcer cried out the loss of my generous brother

verses.sadr: لقد صوت الناعي بفقد اخي الندى
verses.ajuz: نداء لعمري لا ابا لك يسمع
أخو الخمر يسمو تارة ثم يصرع

verses.sadr: فمن لقرى الأضياف بعدك إن هم
كعهدهم اذ انت حي واذ لهم

verses.ajuz: وأمر وهى من صاحب ليس يرقع

verses.sadr: ولو كنت حيا كان اطفاء جهله
وكنت إذا ما خفت إرداف عسرة

verses.ajuz: له موسر ينفى به العسر اجمع

translationEn: The announcer of death cried out the loss of my generous brother — a cry, by my life, that struck the ears with force. I rose while my soul had nearly, at the shock of his death, taken to following him from grief — towards him, as though I bore a sin and was humbled, like a wine-drinker who rises at times then is felled again. Who now is there for the hospitality of guests after you, if they settle before your door and call out to be heard? As was their custom when you were alive and when they had at your hand what they needed, quenching drink and full satisfaction. And who is there for a grave matter that has settled hard upon a neighbour and an affair gone weak for want of someone to mend it? Who for a fellow-sitter who speaks obscenely to his companion, pressing upon him with ignorance and haste? Had you been alive, extinguishing his ignorance would have been done with your forbearance and gentle patience — and your forbearance was broader still. And when I feared the coming of hardship I would spend the day in apprehension and cover myself in dread. I would call upon Sakhr the generous and find in him an abundance that drove away all hardship entirely.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the one who announces a death, the death-crier
my brother of generosity — a possessive epithet of praise
guilt, sin; a burden one carries with remorse
the hospitality of guests, feeding and sheltering travellers
the coming of hardship one after another
to cover oneself, to veil one's face in fear or shame
one in easy circumstances, capable of giving generously

context: An elegy structured around a series of 'who now?' questions, each identifying a social role — host, mediator, protector, counsellor — that Sakhr once fulfilled. The personal closing, where Al-Khansa recalls calling upon Sakhr in her own need, makes this one of the most intimate of her poems.

titleEn: Why does your eye not sleep

verses.ajuz: إذا جر في القد لا يرفع

translationEn: Why does your eye not sleep, weeping as if weeping could avail? Their tears were like pearls falling loose, or even faster than that — rolling down, their string broken, slipping from the thread entirely. Weep for Sakhr and lament none other, for the young man was a born orator. He has gone, and we shall follow him in turn — such is the fate of every young man, his place of falling. He was the warrior ready in arms and the eloquent speaker among his people, the easy-going and the crier of plenty. And a captive scratching his shinbone when dragged in his leather bonds, unable to rise — he called to you and you shredded his chains, though the captive thought before your coming they could never be cut. And a firm, reliable she-camel stripped of her load to be slaughtered for a group of hungry men — she spent the day stumbling on three legs when she had four, struck with a blade so sharp that when you levelled it the bones were like tender stalks before it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to sleep, to find rest (of the eye)
pearls (falling loose — simile for free-falling tears)
the string of a necklace; its breaking scatters the pearls
a naturally gifted orator, eloquent by nature
the shins (of a captive grinding his bones against his bonds)
a rawhide thong or leather rope used to bind captives
a large, sharp sword; blade of the finest quality

context: An elegy notable for its concrete, vivid vignettes: the captive whose bonds Sakhr broke, and the she-camel slaughtered to feed the hungry — illustrating his generosity and strength through specific acts rather than abstract praise.

titleEn: The long night refuses to let me sleep

verses.sadr: نعي ابن عمر و اتى موهنا

translationEn: The great length of my night has refused to let me sleep, and the most horrible piece of news has overcome me — the death-announcement of ibn Amr came at midnight, slain. How should I not be overcome with grief? The treachery of this time has bereaved me of him, and calamities do indeed bereave. The loss of one like my beloved makes eyes weep and pains those who were never pained. He was a brother of whom no companion complained, nor did any of the travelling party complain of his hunger. He would tremble in battle at the time of single combat as a polished sword of fine grain trembles in the hand. What is it to me and this time of recurring misfortunes — does every frightful thing fall upon us in turn?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: refused, would not allow (the night refusing sleep)
overcame me, took hold of me
the most horrible, the most revolting
at midnight, in the middle of the night
that which has fine lustre — said of a polished sword
with fine, distinct grain or watering (of a sword blade)
recurring calamities, afflictions that keep returning

context: An elegy lamenting the death of Sakhr (ibn Amr), centred on the shock of receiving the news at midnight — one of Al-Khansa's recurring motifs of sleeplessness — ending with a despairing rhetorical question about time's relentless cruelty.

titleAr: يا أم عمر و ألا تبكين معولة
titleEn: O mother of Amr, will you not wail

verses.sadr: يا أم عمر و ألا تبكين معولة
verses.ajuz: على اخيك وقد اعلى به الناعي

translationEn: O mother of Amr, will you not weep with wailing cries for your brother, now that the death-crier has proclaimed him loudly? Weep and do not weary of lamentation, bereaved woman, for your brother who was lofty in aspiration and far-reaching in deed. For you have been bereaved of one of blessed fortune in leadership, with many avenues of action, both a cause of harm to enemies and a bringer of benefit to his people. Who is there for us, now that we have lost him and parted from a lord who was a defence at the rear of his people? He was our lord who called his clan together — may he not be far removed, for what a fine lord he was who called!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a woman wailing loudly in grief
the one who announces a death publicly
a bereaved woman who has torn her garments in mourning
one of blessed fortune in leadership, auspicious in his qualities
having many avenues of action, resourceful
one who harms enemies and benefits allies
the one who calls his clan to rally — a role of leadership

context: Al-Khansa addresses her mother, urging her to weep for their fallen brother, cataloguing his qualities as a blessed leader, defender, and caller of the clan. The final verse ends with a blessing that he not be distant, suggesting she is speaking to his spirit.

titleEn: I remembered Sakhr when a dove sang

verses.ajuz: هتوف على غصن من الأيك تسجع
وليس لمن قد غاله الدهر مرجع

verses.sadr: فإن كان صخر الجود أصبح ثاويا
verses.ajuz: فقد كان في الدنيا يضر وينفع

translationEn: I remembered Sakhr when a dove sang — a calling, cooing bird on a branch of the tamarisk, trilling. I spent the time weeping for it with sorrowful tears, my heart aching from what it reminded me of. It reminded me of Sakhr, though between us now lie flat stones and rocks and a vast, barren, ashen wasteland. I see that time shoots arrows that never miss their mark, and there is no return for one whom time has consumed. If Sakhr the generous has now become one who lies still in the earth, he was in this world one who brought harm and brought benefit.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a dove that calls out with a penetrating, moving cry
to coo repeatedly with a rhythmic, mournful sound
flat slabs of stone (covering a grave)
a vast empty desert, bare and desolate
whose arrows never go astray (said of fate's deadly precision)
lying still, dwelling in the grave

context: One of Al-Khansa's most lyrical elegies, opening with the classic Arabic image of a dove's song triggering remembrance of the dead. The poem moves from intimate grief to philosophical resignation, ending with a balanced summary of Sakhr's impact: he brought both harm and benefit in life.

titleEn: I swore I will not cease offering poems

verses.ajuz: لصخر أخي المفضال في كل مجمع

translationEn: I have sworn I shall not cease offering poems to Sakhr, my magnanimous brother in every gathering. May the Sulaym clan ransom you with its elders and its young men, and may every nose and ear of theirs be cut off for your sake.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I shall not cease, I will never stop
to dedicate and offer a poem as a gift
the supremely generous, the one who bestows countless favours
may they be your ransom (an expression of sacrifice and love)
to cut off the nose (an extreme expression of self-humiliation or sacrifice)

context: A very short but solemn vow — Al-Khansa swears to never stop composing poetry in Sakhr's honour, and calls on the Sulaym tribe to sacrifice themselves for him. The cutting of noses and ears is a hyperbolic expression of complete self-abasement in mourning.

titleAr: يا عين بكي بدمع غير إنزاف
titleEn: O eye, weep with inexhaustible tears

verses.sadr: يا عين بكي بدمع غير إنزاف

verses.ajuz: أو صائح في فروع النخل هتاف

verses.sadr: أبي اليتامى إذا ما شتوة نزلت ؛

translationEn: O my eye, weep with tears that never run dry, and weep for Sakhr — no amount of weeping will be sufficient for him. Be like a grey dove in the branches of its forest, or a crying, calling voice in the fronds of the palm trees. Weep for a rain-filled cloud laden with downpour that shook the noblemen when the lesser men grew lax. And weep for the host of guests when a thunderous storm-cloud rushed, throwing down hailstones quick to cause disaster, pounding in torrents. The father of orphans when winter descended, and steadfast in battle without flinching.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: running dry, being completely exhausted (of tears)
a grey dove (symbol of mourning and lamentation)
a dense thicket, a forest where lions or doves dwell
a cloud that fills the horizon, a bank of rain clouds
rain, the rain that falls from a cloud
a thundering, rumbling storm-cloud
one who trembles or flees in fear (in battle)

context: A short elegy comparing Sakhr to a thunderous rain-cloud — a standard epithet of generosity in Arabic poetry — and to a sheltering father for orphans, combining images of natural abundance with martial steadfastness.

titleAr: ما لذا الموت لا يزال مخيفا
titleEn: Why does death remain forever fearsome

verses.sadr: ما لذا الموت لا يزال مخيفا
كان في الحق أن يعود لنا الموت
ايها الموت لو تجافيت عن صخر

translationEn: What ails this death that it remains ever fearsome, reaching one of our noble men every single day? It is consumed with passion for our finest men — it takes none but the most refined and lordly. If only death were to deal justly among us, taking both the noble and the base — it would be right that death come back to us and that we not be kept waiting with false delay. O death, had you kept away from Sakhr, you would have found him pure and morally upright. He lived fifty years among us, forbidding wrongdoing and giving of goodness. May the mercy of God and peace be upon him, and may the spring and autumn rains water his grave.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fearsome, terrifying
the most refined and lordly man, the noblest of nobles
fate, death (poetic feminine term)
procrastination, false delay, putting off
kept away from, gave a wide berth to
morally pure, upright, chaste in character
fifty pilgrimage-years — the Arabic poetic way of counting years

context: The final poem in this batch is notable for its direct address to Death as a character, complaining of its injustice in only taking the best. It closes with one of the most tender lines Al-Khansa ever wrote — a blessing upon Sakhr's grave followed by a prayer for rain, combining Islamic formula with the ancient Bedouin wish for a watered grave.
